medications.indication_rationale: First-line simple analgesia for a Grade 1 sprain; well tolerated and available over the counter.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (maximum 4 g in 24 hours) for up to 48 hours

medications.indication_rationale: Adjunctive analgesic and anti-inflammatory for short-term symptom relief; taken with food to reduce gastric irritation.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally three times daily with food for up to 48 hours

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are not indicated for a mild Grade 1 sprain — simple analgesia is sufficient and opioids carry disproportionate sedation, constipation and dependence risk. Recommending them is an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the RICE protocol with an emphasis on protected EARLY mobilisation — rest briefly, ice for 15-20 minutes every 2 hours (with a damp cloth between ice and skin), a simple tubular compression bandage (Tubigrip) for support, and elevation. Walk as normally as pain allows rather than fully immobilising.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The joint is stable, so early protected movement speeds recovery; rigid immobilisation of a Grade 1 sprain slows healing and weakens proprioception.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Perform gentle active range-of-motion exercises — 'trace the alphabet' with the big toe about three times a day.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Active range-of-motion pumps out swelling and helps align remodelling collagen fibres, reducing stiffness.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take care on the two-storey home's stairs, using the handrails while the ankle is painful.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces the risk of a further fall while gait is antalgic.

patient_education_points.point: You have stretched, but not torn through, the main stabilising ligament on the outside of the ankle. Because the joint is stable, fully immobilising it would actually slow recovery — walk as normally as the pain allows.

An x-ray is not needed tonight: you can bear weight and there is no tenderness over the specific ankle and foot bones, so a fracture is very unlikely by the Ottawa Ankle Rules. Imaging is only needed if things do not settle or a warning sign appears.
When icing, put a damp cloth between the ice pack and your skin and limit it to 15-20 minutes at a time to avoid ice burn.
Most Grade 1 sprains recover within 1-3 weeks with early, gentle movement.

follow_up_plan.interval: physiotherapy review in 5-7 days; GP review if not improving within 7-10 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: pain, swelling, weight-bearing and ankle stability; progress to strengthening and proprioceptive (balance) rehabilitation; consider delayed x-ray or ultrasound if not improving to look for occult bony injury or higher-grade ligament tear
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: inability to bear weight, new bony point tenderness, rapidly increasing swelling, or pain that dramatically worsens despite regular analgesia

must_include_items: Recognise a likely minor lateral ankle ligament sprain and reassure appropriately
Confirm the reassuring features via patient self-assessment — able to bear weight / take four steps, and no bony point tenderness (guided Ottawa Ankle Rules)
Advise conservative management: RICE with protected EARLY mobilisation, not full immobilisation
Advise simple analgesia (paracetamol, with or without ibuprofen)
Provide safety-netting for the features that would change the plan (see errors_of_omission) and arrange physiotherapy / GP follow-up
acceptable_alternatives: Guiding the patient through a structured Ottawa Ankle Rules self-check and, on that basis, advising same-day imaging ONLY if weight-bearing is not possible or bony point tenderness is present
Recommending paracetamol alone, or topical NSAID, as reasonable analgesic alternatives
Advising a short period of relative rest before mobilising, provided early movement is still encouraged
errors_of_omission: No safety-netting for neurovascular compromise (cold, pale, or numb foot)
No safety-netting for inability to bear weight or new bony point tenderness (fracture)
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a weight-bearing, Ottawa-negative sprain to the emergency department or calling 000
Ordering / insisting on an x-ray tonight despite a negative Ottawa screen
Recommending a cast, walking boot, or strict non-weight-bearing immobilisation for a stable Grade 1 sprain
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a genuinely minor, self-limiting injury: conservative self-care plus clear safety-netting IS the correct answer and passes. Over-triaging to urgent in-person assessment / ED / 000 for a weight-bearing Ottawa-negative sprain is a defect (over-triage), as is over-immobilisation. A pass requires conservative management with safety-netting; failing to safety-net the neurovascular and fracture red flags is the main under-triage failure.
